region_of_origin: India (North Indian / Mughlai tradition); served in Singapore

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for tandoori-marinated grilled tiger prawns. FOODON_03302757 (shrimp food product) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP covers cooked crustaceans broadly. No specific code exists for tandoori-marinated grilled tiger prawns; closest_parent applied.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226888001 identifies prawn as a substance. No specific concept for tandoori preparation exists; closest_parent applied.

serving.natural_language_portion: A half portion of Tandoori Jheenga Jal Taran typically comprises 4 to 5 large tiger prawns (approximately 200 g edible weight after shell removal), served with mint chutney and lemon wedges.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Prawns, cooked (tandoori-style grilled crustacean, marinated with spices and yoghurt)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN values for cooked prawns (Penaeus monodon / tiger prawn) were used as the primary nutritional anchor. Adjustments were applied for the tandoori marinade (yoghurt, spices, oil) based on USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for plain low-fat yoghurt (NDB 01116) and spice blends, following standard recipe-level estimation. The half-portion designation refers to serving size only and does not alter per-100g values.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — seafood and protein-dominant foods
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for tandoori jheenga. GI is estimated at 5 based on the University of Sydney GI Database classification of cooked crustaceans and high-protein, very-low-carbohydrate foods as having negligible glycemic impact (GI < 10). The marinade contributes minimal carbohydrate (~2.8 g per 100 g), yielding a glycemic load of approximately 0.3 per 200 g serving. gi_category is 'low' per the ≤55 threshold rule.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from marinade spices and preparation
hypertension.guidance: Tandoori jheenga contains approximately 420 mg sodium per 100 g, primarily from spice blends and marinade; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to one serving and avoid additional salt or high-sodium condiments such as pickles or chutneys.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content and high protein
type2_diabetes.guidance: With only 2.8 g carbohydrate per 100 g and a glycemic index estimated at 5, tandoori jheenga has negligible glycemic impact and is a suitable high-protein, low-GI choice for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, provided accompanying bread or rice portions are controlled.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Lean protein with moderate cholesterol; low saturated fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Tandoori jheenga provides lean seafood protein with only 1.4 g saturated fat per 100 g and beneficial omega-3 fatty acids from tiger prawns; while dietary cholesterol is moderate at 152 mg per 100 g, current evidence does not support restricting dietary cholesterol in cardiovascular disease management, and this dish is a heart-healthier alternative to fried or cream-based prawn preparations.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tandoori Jheenga Jal Taran is a North Indian tandoor-grilled tiger prawn dish in which large Penaeus monodon prawns are marinated in a blend of yoghurt, red chilli, cumin, coriander, ginger, garlic, lemon juice, and proprietary spice mixes before being cooked at high temperature in a traditional clay oven (tandoor). The intense dry heat of the tandoor imparts a characteristic smoky char on the exterior while keeping the prawn flesh succulent and juicy inside. The dish is typically served with mint-coriander chutney, sliced onion rings, and lemon wedges. In Singapore, it is offered at North Indian restaurants such as Kinara at Boat Quay as a starter or sharing plate. Nutritionally, tandoori jheenga is a high-protein, low-carbohydrate seafood dish providing approximately 20.5 g protein and only 2.8 g carbohydrate per 100 g, with a glycemic index estimated at 5 — making it suitable for low-GI dietary patterns and individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. The fat content is moderate at 5.8 g per 100 g, predominantly unsaturated. Sodium is a consideration at 420 mg per 100 g due to the spice marinade. The dish is naturally gluten-free and halal-compliant. It is rich in selenium, vitamin B12, phosphorus, and iodine from the prawn base, supporting thyroid function and neurological health. The half-portion serving (approximately 200 g, 4–5 prawns) delivers roughly 296 kcal.
common_user_queries: How many calories in tandoori prawns?
Is tandoori jheenga good for diabetics?
What is Jal Taran at Kinara?
Can I eat tandoori prawns with high blood pressure?
What spices are in tandoori jheenga marinade?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN cooked prawn data with recipe-level adjustments for the tandoori marinade (yoghurt, spices, oil) using USDA SR Legacy. No exact match for this specific restaurant preparation exists in any approved primary source. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database principles for high-protein, very-low-carbohydrate seafood. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN cooked prawn dataset for this preparation type. 2. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for cooked crustaceans. 3. Trans fat (trans_fat_g) is 0.0; confirmed zero as tiger prawns and the marinade ingredients (yoghurt, vegetable oil, spices) do not contain industrially produced trans fats; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for these ingredient classes. 4. The 'half' designation in the food name refers to a half-portion serving size (approximately 200 g, 4–5 tiger prawns) relative to a full restaurant order; per-100g nutrient values are unaffected. 5. Sodium estimate of 420 mg per 100 g reflects the combined contribution of the spice marinade and natural prawn sodium; actual restaurant sodium may vary. 6. GI is estimated at 5 (effectively negligible) based on University of Sydney GI Database analogous classification for cooked crustaceans and very-low-carbohydrate protein foods; gi_category is 'low' per the ≤55 threshold rule. 7. Halal status is assumed based on the restaurant's North Indian Muslim culinary tradition; formal halal certification should be verified with Kinara directly.

Populated nutrient values from ICMR-NIN with USDA SR Legacy marinade adaptation.
Set GI to 5 (analogous estimate, University of Sydney GI Database) with gi_category 'low'.
Documented all -1 sentinel values (vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg) in notes per G3 rule.

Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases are perfectly consistent per Q8 rule.
